title: Alerting on Pipeline Health
concept: Setting thresholds on latency, error rate, and retrieval confidence to catch production incidents automatically.

exercises.prompt: Why do we implement user feedback loops in production LLM applications?

options.text: To instantly block unauthorized IP addresses from accessing the LLM.
To collect ratings and corrections to build a dataset for evaluation and fine-tuning.
To automatically rewrite user prompts before they reach the model.

exercises.explain: User feedback loops capture direct ratings (like thumbs-up or down) and raw text corrections, which help you build datasets for evaluations and fine-tuning.

cards.title: Catching Fires Early
cards.body: We've built feedback loops to learn from users over time, but what happens when the pipeline suddenly breaks at 2 AM? We need automated lookouts to wake us up before our users notice the failure.

cards.title: Pipeline Health Alerting
cards.body: Alerting on pipeline health means setting up automated triggers that ping your engineering team when metrics like system response time, API error rates, or database retrieval scores cross a safe threshold.

cards.title: The Smoke Detector Rule
cards.body: Think of alerting like a smoke detector in a kitchen. You don't want it going off every time you toast bread (false positive), but if there's actual fire, you need a loud siren immediately before the whole house fills with smoke.

cards.title: Thresholds in Action
cards.body: If your RAG pipeline's average retrieval confidence score drops below 0.60 for five minutes straight, an automated system sends a Slack alert to the team to check if the database index is corrupted.

cards.title: The Cry-Wolf Trap
cards.body: Some believe you should alert on every single minor error. In reality, setting alerts too sensitive causes 'alert fatigue,' where developers ignore the constant notifications and miss real critical failures.

cards.title: Healthy vs. Unhealthy Pipelines
cards.body: Choose the right metrics to monitor so you can distinguish between normal operations and system failures.

left.text: Requests taking 10+ seconds. Users are staring at loading spinners. Alert the team!

right.text: Occasional 2-second queries during peak hours. No action needed yet.

exercises.prompt: What does a sudden drop in the 'retrieval confidence' metric typically indicate in a RAG pipeline?

options.text: The API billing account has run out of funds.
The database retrieval system is returning highly irrelevant documents.
Users are writing inputs in a language the LLM does not understand.
The LLM's response length has exceeded the maximum token limit.

exercises.explain: Retrieval confidence indicates how well the system found matching documents. A sudden drop means the search query or database is returning irrelevant junk to the LLM.

exercises.prompt: True or False: You should configure alerts to notify developers immediately every time a single query takes longer than 2 seconds.
exercises.explain: Alerts should target actionable issues that degrade the user experience. Alerting on every tiny spike leads to alert fatigue and causes teams to ignore real warnings.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the description of automated incident detection.
exercises.sentence: We set limits called thresholds on our system metrics to automatically trigger alarms when things break.
exercises.explain: Setting thresholds defines the boundary between normal operation and an active incident, preventing system failures from going unnoticed.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following configurations is an AI-generated alerting rule for monitoring system health?

options.text: Trigger critical alert if 'error_rate > 0.05' for a duration of '5m' on service 'rag-llm-pipeline'.
Hey team, I noticed the server seems a bit laggy today. Let me know if you see any errors in the console.
Weekly report: User satisfaction is steady at 94% positive thumbs-up ratings.

exercises.explain: Option B mimics a human developer writing a standard log message. Option A is an actual alert rule configuration setting a threshold on error rates over a time window, which is typical for production systems. Option C is a general user feedback summary.

exercises.prompt: How does setting up automated alerts on pipeline health metrics (like latency and retrieval confidence) connect with your user feedback loop?

options.text: Alerting is useless because user feedback loops will eventually tell us if something is broken.
Operational alerts detect system failures in real-time before bad outputs degrade user feedback metrics.
You should only set alerts on user-submitted ratings to avoid spamming the developers.
Retrieval confidence is a subjective metric and cannot be used for automated alerting.

exercises.explain: A spike in user thumbs-down ratings is a symptom, but measuring latency, error rates, and low-confidence retrieval thresholds helps you catch the disease (like database failures or degraded APIs) before users even notice and click the thumbs-down button.
